perturbation.original_text: In the event any Representative of a Party is permitted at any time to visit any of the business or project sites of another Party or any of its Affiliates, such visiting Party shall indemnify and save such other Party harmless from and against any and all claims and liabilities, including actual out-of-pocket costs and expenses for loss, injury to or death of any such person, and any loss, damage to or destruction of any property, incurred by such other Party or any of its Affiliates resulting directly or indirectly from the negligence or misconduct of any of such Representatives during any such visit.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event any Representative of a Party visits the sites of another Party, the visiting Party agrees to consider indemnifying the other Party for significant claims arising from gross negligence directly attributable to those Representatives during the visit. The Parties will endeavor to resolve such matters equitably.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a strict indemnification clause. The revised text weakens this obligation by (1) using "agrees to consider indemnifying", (2) limiting indemnification to only "significant claims arising from gross negligence directly attributable to those Representatives during the visit", and (3) using 'endeavor to resolve such matters equitably', which opens it up to subjective interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original language of the contract provided for indemnification for injury or death. The modified contract does not. By reducing indemnification coverage to only 'significant claims arising from gross negligence' it makes it less likely that the company will be indemnified for injuries or deaths that may result. This potentially forces the visited company to rely on worker's compensation, and if the representative of the visiting party was at fault, the visiting party should indemnify the visited party.

Back to Act Listing By Act Name Printer Friendly Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. (820 ILCS 305/1) (from Ch. 48, par. 138.1) Sec. 1. This Act may be cited as the Workers' Compensation Act. (a) The term "employer" as used in this Act means: 1. The State and each county, city, town, township, incorporated village, school district, body politic, or municipal corporation therein. 2. Every person, firm, public or private corporation, including hospitals, public service, eleemosynary, religious or charitable corporations or associations who has any person in service or under any contract for hire, express or implied, oral or written, and who is engaged in any of the enterprises or businesses enumerated in Section 3 of this Act, or who at or prior to the time of the accident to the employee for which compensation under this Act may be claimed, has in the manner provided in this Act elected to become subject to the provisions of this Act, and who has not, prior to such accident, effected a withdrawal of such election in the manner provided in this Act. 3. Any one engaging in any business or enterprise referred to in subsections 1 and 2 of Sect
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the indemnification clause. The original text mandates indemnification for 'any and all claims and liabilities,' while the revised text states the visiting party 'agrees to consider indemnifying' only for 'significant claims arising from gross negligence.' The original text covered loss, injury or death, while the modified one does not. The original text says the visiting party 'shall indemnify and save such other Party harmless', while the changed text says 'The Parties will endeavor to resolve such matters equitably.' This shift introduces ambiguity and reduces the visiting party's obligation, potentially leaving the visited party vulnerable to losses and liabilities without clear recourse. The provided law snippet discusses worker's compensation, and the law explanation mentions that the visited party could be forced to rely on this in the case of injury or death, rather than relying on the visiting party to indemnify them. Since the original contract provided for indemnification for injury or death, and the modified contract does not, a legal contradiction is created.
